itunes_results.name: Brilliant — Math & Science
itunes_results.note: Interactive problem-solving platform — not a daily streak app narrating calculus history: Newton vs Leibniz priority dispute, fluxions vs infinitesimals, Berkeley's 'The Analyst' critique, Cauchy's rigorization

itunes_results.name: Khan Academy
itunes_results.note: General math instruction platform — zero narrative on the historical controversy, mathematical revolutions, or personalities behind calculus

daily_history_of_calculus_streak.gap_note: 3.0M combined community. Newton vs Leibniz is the most famous intellectual priority dispute in science. The story of calculus — from Archimedes' method of exhaustion, through Cavalieri's indivisibles, to the Newton-Leibniz war, to Cauchy's epsilon-delta rigorization — is genuinely gripping. No streak app tells this story daily. STEM students, math lovers, science history fans would pay for bite-sized episodes.

itunes_results.name: Photomath
itunes_results.note: Camera-based math solver — solves equations, zero historical context about Euclid's proof of infinite primes, Fermat's Last Theorem 358-year journey, Riemann Hypothesis

itunes_results.name: Mathway
itunes_results.note: Math problem solver — no historical narrative of Gauss's Disquisitiones Arithmeticae, Ramanujan's taxicab numbers, Goldbach's unproven conjecture

daily_number_theory_history_streak.gap_note: 2.9M combined community. Number theory has the best stories in mathematics: Fermat's 358-year unsolved Last Theorem, Ramanujan's miraculous intuition, the 160-year-old Riemann Hypothesis, twin primes conjecture. Euclid's infinity-of-primes proof is 2,300 years old and still elegant. Zero streak apps teach number theory history. Pure math enthusiasts and puzzle lovers are underserved.

itunes_results.name: Daily Biography — Famous People
itunes_results.note: General biography app covering politicians/celebrities — mathematicians get one entry among thousands, no depth on their actual work or context

itunes_results.name: Anki Flashcards
itunes_results.note: Spaced repetition tool — no curated narrative content on Euler, Gauss, Ramanujan, Emmy Noether, Alan Turing, Sophie Germain stories

daily_famous_mathematicians_streak.gap_note: 19.9M combined community. Euler published 866 papers and lost his eyesight at 59 but kept working by memory. Ramanujan taught himself from a single textbook and rediscovered 100 years of mathematics before dying at 32. Emmy Noether proved theorems while banned from lecturing under Nazism. These stories are more gripping than most biographies. No streak app delivers a mathematician-per-day format. History lovers + STEM students = huge overlap.

itunes_results.name: GeoGebra Geometry
itunes_results.note: Interactive geometry drawing tool — zero historical narrative on Euclid's Elements, non-Euclidean geometry revolutions (Lobachevsky, Riemann), projective geometry emergence

itunes_results.name: Geometry Dash
itunes_results.note: Rhythm platformer game — not educational geometry at all

daily_history_of_geometry_streak.gap_note: 3.1M combined community. Euclid's Elements is the second most-printed book in history after the Bible. Non-Euclidean geometry's discovery by Bolyai, Lobachevsky, and Riemann shattered 2,000 years of certainty and unlocked Einstein's relativity. Hilbert's axiomization of geometry. Fractal geometry by Mandelbrot. This history is intellectually transformative and completely untapped in streak format. Architecture and design communities would also convert.

itunes_results.name: Simon Singh — The Code Book (audiobook)
itunes_results.note: Audiobook of the classic cryptography history — passive consumption, no gamification, no streak, no daily micro-lesson format

itunes_results.name: Cryptogram Puzzles
itunes_results.note: Solve substitution cipher puzzles — zero historical narrative on Caesar, Vigenere, Enigma, one-time pads, RSA invention

daily_cryptography_history_streak.gap_note: 17.4M combined community. Cryptography history is extraordinary: Caesar cipher → Al-Kindi's frequency analysis (800 AD) → Vigenere's 'unbreakable' cipher → Babbage's hidden breakthrough → WWI Room 40 → Enigma and Bletchley Park → public key cryptography invented by Diffie-Hellman in 1976. Tech professionals, security engineers, and history fans all want this. No dedicated streak app exists. Crossover appeal with both history AND security audiences.

itunes_results.name: Philosophy Breaks — Daily Wisdom
itunes_results.note: General philosophy app — does not cover mathematical logic revolution: Boole, Frege, Russell's Paradox, Godel's incompleteness theorems, Church-Turing thesis

itunes_results.name: Logic Games — Brain Training
itunes_results.note: Puzzle/brain training — no historical content on how Godel proved mathematics cannot prove its own consistency, upending Hilbert's program

daily_mathematical_logic_streak.gap_note: 10.2M combined community. Godel's incompleteness theorems (1931) are the most profound mathematical results of the 20th century — they proved that any sufficiently powerful mathematical system contains truths it cannot prove. Russell's paradox destroyed naive set theory. Turing's halting problem is the conceptual foundation of computer science. These ideas span math, philosophy, AND computer science. Three overlapping communities with no dedicated streak app serving any of them on this specific topic.

itunes_results.name: Statistics — Pocket Reference
itunes_results.note: Formula reference tool — no historical context on Pascal-Fermat probability correspondence, Bayes' posthumous theorem, Gauss inventing least squares at 18, Fisher vs Pearson feud

itunes_results.name: Learn Statistics & Data Analysis
itunes_results.note: Instruction-based course app — zero narrative on how Florence Nightingale invented the pie chart to save soldier lives, how Galton invented regression while studying heredity

daily_statistics_history_streak.gap_note: 6.8M combined community. Statistics history is counterintuitively gripping: Pascal and Fermat invented probability theory in 1654 correspondence about gambling debts. Bayes' theorem was published posthumously by a friend. Florence Nightingale pioneered data visualization to convince the British Army to fix hospital sanitation. Fisher and Pearson's vicious rivalry shaped modern hypothesis testing. Data scientists and ML practitioners lack historical literacy about their own field's origins — this app fills that gap with clear commercial intent.

itunes_results.name: Photomath
itunes_results.note: Equation solver — zero historical content on Al-Khwarizmi coining 'algebra' (al-jabr), Cardano's cubic formula, Galois dying at 20 having invented group theory, Abel's impossibility proof

itunes_results.name: Algebra Touch
itunes_results.note: Interactive algebra manipulation tool — solves equations, no history of the word 'algebra' originating in 9th-century Baghdad or the Renaissance formula wars

daily_history_of_algebra_streak.gap_note: 19.1M combined community. Algebra's history is a 3,000-year thriller: Babylonian tablets to Al-Khwarizmi's Baghdad House of Wisdom, Italian Renaissance duels over cubic formulas (Tartaglia vs Cardano), 19-year-old Galois inventing group theory the night before his fatal duel, Abel proving the quintic unsolvable at 22. This progression from arithmetic to abstract algebra is the backbone of all modern mathematics. History and math communities are both large — no streak app connects them.

itunes_results.name: Daily Puzzles — Brain Teasers
itunes_results.note: Puzzle game app — no historical context on Euler's Konigsberg bridges (birth of graph theory), Fibonacci's rabbit problem, the 15-puzzle craze of 1880, Rubik's Cube mathematical group theory

itunes_results.name: Math Riddles and Puzzles
itunes_results.note: Collection of math puzzles — no narrative framing, no historical origin stories, no context about how recreational mathematics often leads to serious mathematics

daily_recreational_math_history_streak.gap_note: 4.9M combined community. Recreational mathematics is the gateway drug to serious math. Euler invented graph theory to solve the Konigsberg bridge problem as a party curiosity. Fibonacci's rabbit problem introduced the sequence while writing a 13th-century merchant arithmetic manual. The 15-puzzle created a national craze in 1880 and launched combinatorics research. Martin Gardner's Scientific American column introduced millions to mathematics. A daily streak app delivering the ORIGIN STORY of famous puzzles — not just the puzzles themselves — occupies a unique niche with no direct competition.

itunes_results.name: 3Blue1Brown (YouTube companion)
itunes_results.note: Visualization companion — not a streak app, video-based, no daily lesson format on Mobius strips, Klein bottles, Poincare Conjecture proof

itunes_results.name: Brilliant — Math
itunes_results.note: Interactive courses — does not cover history of topology: Euler's polyhedron formula, Mobius and the one-sided surface, Poincare's 100-year conjecture, Perelman refusing the $1M prize

daily_topology_history_streak.gap_note: 3.7M combined community. Topology has the most visually arresting and philosophically strange results in mathematics. Euler's V-E+F=2 formula (1750) is topology's origin. Mobius discovered his strip while working on a German government surveying problem. Poincare's conjecture about 3-spheres was unsolved for 99 years until Perelman proved it — then refused the $1M Millennium Prize and the Fields Medal. These stories are objectively remarkable. A daily topology-history streak app would be uniquely positioned at the intersection of visual math and science storytelling.
